frameworks.name: decision criterion updating
frameworks.one_line: Each new stimulus gradually shifts the subject's decision boundary.

frameworks.name: sensory history / serial dependence
frameworks.one_line: Trial-by-trial stimulus history biases the current perceptual judgment.

core_question: How does recent stimulus history shape judgments of tactile vibration strength on the current trial?
key_assumptions: Judgment can be modeled as comparison of stimulus to a decision criterion
Sensory history exerts its bias independent of prior choice or reward
A single mechanism can capture both rat and human history effects
manipulations: Stimulus mean speed on trial n-1 (low vs high)
Time interval between trial n-1 and trial n
Species (rat vibrissal vs human fingertip vibration)
key_findings: Low-speed n-1 stimulus biases trial n toward 'strong' (repulsive aftereffect)
History effect is independent of previous choice and reward outcome
Effect decays exponentially across multiple preceding trials
Influence of trial n-1 strengthens with longer inter-trial interval; humans replicate the same pattern
interpretations: Each new stimulus incrementally updates the decision criterion
A single-parameter criterion-update model accounts for the repulsive history effect and its time scale
